talk_title: Multimodal and Agentic AI in Biomarker Discovery

speaker.name: Jakob Kata
speaker.affiliation: University of Dresden and University of Heidelberg, Germany

MOA_summary: Kata described the evolution from classical machine learning to deep learning and foundation models for cancer biomarker development, defining three classes of AI‑based biomarkers (quantification, indirect inference, and outcome prediction) and introduced AI agents that can autonomously integrate multimodal clinical data to generate diagnostic and therapeutic recommendations.
key_findings: Class A AI biomarkers replicate human scoring (e.g., PD‑L1 IHC) with higher reproducibility.
Class B AI biomarkers infer genetic alterations (e.g., MSI, EGFR mutations) from pathology images for pre‑screening.
Class C AI biomarkers predict treatment outcomes directly from multimodal data, enabling new clinical decision tools.

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Stage Programs at Recursion

speaker.name: Tirup Behanie
speaker.affiliation: Recursion Pharmaceuticals, VP of Translational Strategy

targets.dependency_context: ovarian cancer with high CDK7 expression

biomarkers.name: MSI‑high status
biomarkers.finding: MSI‑high ovarian models showed dose‑dependent RBM39 degradation and greater sensitivity to REC‑1245

biomarkers.name: CDK7 expression
biomarkers.finding: High CDK7 expression correlated with poorer overall survival in thousands of Tempus patient records

biomarkers.name: Polyp burden change
biomarkers.finding: 75% of FAP patients responded with median 43% reduction after 12 weeks of REC‑4881; 82% maintained response (53% reduction) 12 weeks off therapy

models.name: DAHLIA (REC‑1245) Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with MSI‑high or high genomic instability tumors

models.name: ELUCIDATE (REC‑617) Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced solid tumors; ovarian cancer cohort

clinical_data.population: FAP patients treated 12 weeks with REC‑4881

clinical_data.n: n=1 ovarian cancer patient in ELUCIDATE

MOA_summary: Recursion leveraged AI‑driven phenomaps to identify RBM39 as a functional analog of CDK12, enabling rapid degrader development (REC‑1245) that exploits splicing defects in MSI‑high, genomically unstable tumors; AI‑guided design produced a CDK7 inhibitor (REC‑617) optimized for ovarian cancer vulnerability; and AI‑derived repurposing identified a MEK1/2 inhibitor (REC‑4881) that reduces polyp burden in APC‑loss FAP patients.
key_findings: RBM39 degradation by REC‑1245 showed dose‑dependent activity in MSI‑high ovarian models and correlated with genomic instability across multiple preclinical models.
REC‑1245 entered the DAHLIA Phase 1/2 trial targeting MSI‑high and other high‑instability tumor types, with dose escalation ongoing.

REC‑617 demonstrated in vitro ovarian cell line growth inhibition and in vivo tumor regressions; causal modeling of >10,000 Tempus records linked high CDK7 expression to poor survival, guiding ovarian cancer selection.
A partial response was observed in an ovarian cancer patient treated with REC‑617 in the ELUCIDATE trial.
REC‑4881 achieved a 75% response rate and 43% median polyp burden reduction after 12 weeks in FAP patients, with 82% maintaining response (53% reduction) after a drug‑free interval.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RBM39 degraders may be more selective than CDK12 inhibitors that also hit CDK13, potentially improving therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: to enhance efficacy in ovarian cancer beyond monotherapy

open_questions: What biomarker thresholds (e.g., MSI‑high vs. quantitative genomic instability scores) best predict response to RBM39 degradation?
Will CDK7 expression become a prospective selection marker for REC‑617 based on larger patient datasets?

talk_title: AI Transformation of Pharmaceutical R&D at AstraZeneca

speaker.name: George Reis-Filho
speaker.affiliation: AstraZeneca, Chief AI and Science Innovation

biomarkers.name: Normalized membrane ratio (NMR)
biomarkers.assay: quantitative continuous scoring on pathology images
biomarkers.finding: NMR correlates with Trop2 internalization and predicts response to Trop2‑targeted ADCs in NSCLC

MOA_summary: Reis‑Filho outlined AstraZeneca's strategy to fine‑tune large multimodal foundation models with proprietary oncology data, enabling AI‑driven patient selection (e.g., Trop2 NMR) and accelerating ADC development, while highlighting the shift from handcrafted annotation to foundation‑model‑based quantification that reduced annotation effort from 40,000 to <5 hours.
key_findings: A quantitative continuous scoring system can measure subcellular Trop2 expression and derive a normalized membrane ratio that serves as a surrogate for ADC internalization.
Development of the Trop2 ADC biomarker required ~40,000 pathologist annotation hours for one indication; foundation models can reduce this to <5 hours or eliminate it.
AstraZeneca is building multimodal foundation models through partnerships (Tempus, Pathos, Model AI) to improve patient selection across diverse therapeutic classes.
open_questions: How can multimodal foundation models integrate time‑dimension data to improve longitudinal patient selection?
What validation frameworks are needed to satisfy regulators for AI‑derived biomarkers with limited explainability?
